pattern: First flood the grid with a multi-source BFS from every thief to know how safe each cell is, then run a widest-path (bottleneck) search that prefers the safest neighbor instead of the shortest one.
hints: Compute, for every cell, its distance to the nearest thief first - a single multi-source BFS starting from all thief cells at once gives you this in one pass.
Once every cell has a safety value, the problem becomes: find a path from corner to corner maximizing the MINIMUM safety value visited, not the shortest path.

Run a Dijkstra-like sweep with a max-heap keyed on the best safety reachable so far: pop the most-safe frontier cell, relax neighbors with min(current safety, neighbor's own distance-to-thief), and stop once the destination is finalized.

edge_inputs.description: thieves sit in both corners so every path is forced through danger
a single thief tucked in the far corner still leaves room to keep distance
two thieves on opposite corners squeeze the middle corridor
a thief dead center of an open grid rewards hugging the border
single cell grid, which must itself be the thief
smallest 2x2 grid with the destination cell a thief
the start cell itself is a thief, capping every path at zero regardless of the rest of the grid
the destination cell itself is a thief
a wall of thieves along one row forces the path away from the shortest diagonal route

stress.description: large sparse grid with scattered thieves
stress.note: n=400 with ~3% thief density exercises full-size BFS and the bottleneck sweep

stress.description: large grid with a single thief far from both endpoints
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(400, size))
    grid = [[0] * n for _ in range(n)]
    grid[n // 2][n // 2] = 1
    return [grid]
stress.note: a lone central thief maximizes the safety values the sweep has to compare
